coredata.dc:title: Carboxymethyl cellulose-based multifunctional film integrated with polyphenol-rich extract and carbon dots from coffee husk waste for active food packaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Junxian
Li, Yongshi
Liu, Bingzhen
Wang, Kun
Li, Hui
Peng, Lincai
coredata.dc:description: Sustainable carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC)-based active composite films were developed through the addition of polyphenol-rich extract from coffee husk (CHE) and carbon dots (CDs) prepared using the biowaste residue of CHE extraction. The influences of various CDs contents on the physicochemical and functional characteristics of composite films have been researched. The 6% (w/w) CHE and 3% (w/w) CDs were uniformly dispersed within the CMC matrix to produce a homogenous film with enhanced mechanical properties. The CMC/CHE/CDs3% film exhibited outstanding UV-light blocking, improved water and gas barriers, potent antioxidant activity with above 95% DPPH and ABTS scavenging rates, and effective antibacterial capabilities against L. monocytogenes and E. coli. The food packaging experiment demonstrated that this active composite film slowed the rotting of fresh-cut apples and extended their shelf-life to 7 days at 4 °C storage. Therefore, the obtained multifunctional film showed promise as an environmentally friendly food packaging material.

Carboxymethyl cellulose

head.ce:title: Carboxymethyl cellulose-based multifunctional film integrated with polyphenol-rich extract and carbon dots from coffee husk waste for active food packaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Food Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650500, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sustainable carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC)-based active composite films were developed through the addition of polyphenol-rich extract from coffee husk (CHE) and carbon dots (CDs) prepared using the biowaste residue of CHE extraction. The influences of various CDs contents on the physicochemical and functional characteristics of composite films have been researched. The 6% (w/w) CHE and 3% (w/w) CDs were uniformly dispersed within the CMC matrix to produce a homogenous film with enhanced mechanical properties. The CMC/CHE/CDs3% film exhibited outstanding UV-light blocking, improved water and gas barriers, potent antioxidant activity with above 95% DPPH and ABTS scavenging rates, and effective antibacterial capabilities against L. monocytogenes and E. coli. The food packaging experiment demonstrated that this active composite film slowed the rotting of fresh-cut apples and extended their shelf-life to 7 days at 4 °C storage. Therefore, the obtained multifunctional film showed promise as an environmentally friendly food packaging material.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, nondegradable plastics like polypropylene (PE), polyethylene terephthalate (PET), and polyvinyl chloride (PVC) are widely used in food packaging because of their affordability, favorable mechanical strength, and desirable barrier capabilities (Kumar, Sadeghi, Jang, & Seo, 2023). However, heavy consumption of nondegradable plastics has resulted in high carbon emissions and serious “white pollution”, endangering the environment and public health (Meereboer, Misra, & Mohanty, 2020). In order to mitigate those negative consequences, there is an urgent need to replace traditional plastic packaging with more sustainable alternatives. Biodegradable and active packaging films derived from natural biopolymers emerge as a highly attractive solution due to their appealing advantages, which could effectively prevent food from disrupting its surroundings, such as light, moisture, air, and microbes, thus delaying food spoilage and extending shelf life (Akoueson et al., 2023). Carboxymethylcellulose (CMC), an anionic polysaccharide generated by the introduction of –CH2COOH groups into the cellulose molecular chain, is widely used in the manufacture of biodegradable packaging films because of its renewability, biodegradability, zero toxicity, abundant availability, and good film-forming capacity (Thivya, Bhosale, Anandakumar, Hema, & Sinija, 2022). Despite numerous studies that have demonstrated that pure CMC films possess remarkable transparency, moderate tensile strength, and certain flexibility, their shortcomings including poor UV shielding, high water sensitivity, and the absence of antioxidant and antibacterial capabilities, strongly restrict their potential for use in the food packaging field (Jamróz, Tkaczewska, Kopeć, & Cholewa-Wójcik, 2022; Li, Shi, He, Fei, & Peng, 2020).

Commonly, the most followed strategy to address the above-mentioned issues is adding functional nano-reinforced fillers to the CMC matrix (Jamróz et al., 2022; Thivya et al., 2022). Currently, carbon dots (CDs), as a new class of functional nanofillers, are nearly spherical carbon nanomaterials with a diameter of below 10 nm. They have received considerable interest from researchers in food packaging applications due to their outstanding features, including hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, sustainability, low toxicity, chemical inertness, good compatibility, and high functionality like excellent antioxidant, antibacterial, and fluorescence properties (Oladzadabbasabadi et al., 2023). Numerous methods, such as hydrothermal carbonization, electrochemical method, laser ablation technique, microwave-assisted synthesis, ultrasonic method, atmospheric plasma-based synthesis, have been proposed for the production of CDs (Liu et al., 2024). Among them, the hydrothermal carbonization method is widely adopted due to its ease of operation, high cost-effectiveness, environmental friendliness, and good dispersion in an aqueous solution (Liu et al., 2024; Oladzadabbasabadi et al., 2023). Various agri-biomass wastes are considered good carbon sources to prepare CDs, which not only bring high economic added value but also alleviate environmental pollution. Until now, a variety of biomass waste precursors such as citrus peel (Gong et al., 2023), banana peel (Sul, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023), and Kohlrabi peel (Khan, Riahi, Tae Kim, & Rhim, 2024) have been utilized to successfully synthesize CDs with diverse sizes and properties using the hydrothermal method, and various physicochemical performances of biopolymer-based films (such as UV-light blocking, gas barrier and mechanical strength) have been effectively improved through addition of produced CDs to be used for food packaging.

Coffee is one of the most popular beverages in the world, with a total annual consumption of approximately 1 billion kilograms worldwide (Febrianto & Zhu, 2023). Coffee husk waste (CHW), a main by-product of coffee processing, accounts for 20% of the dry weight of coffee beans with husk. A great majority of CHW is burned, composted, or arbitrarily discarded after coffee processing, which not only delivers minimal added value but also causes second environmental pollution. CHW possesses nearly 85% carbohydrates (Hoseini, Cocco, Casucci, Cardelli, & Corti, 2021), making it an excellent carbon dot precursor. However, there have been no reports on synthesis of CDs derived from coffee husk waste. Moreover, it has also been reported that CHW is rich in bioactive polyphenolic compounds, such as gallic acid, tannic acid, chlorogenic acid, and epicatechin (Hoseini et al., 2021; Silva et al., 2024), which exhibit antioxidant and antibacterial activities and have the potential to serve as bioactive fillers in the development of functional packaging. An antioxidant thermoplastic corn starch film has been developed by addition of active extract from CHW (Collazo-Bigliardi et al., 2019). Based on the cascade biorefinery system (Pardilhó et al., 2023), which is one of the most attractive strategies for the integrated use of waste biomass resources, involving successive treated processes to maximize the yield of products and benefits, we proposed that the CHW is first treated for the production of bioactive polyphenolic extract (i.e., CHE), and then the remaining residue served as a carbon source is further subjected to hydrothermal treatment to synthesize functional CHW-based CDs. With this design, it is believed that CMC-based films incorporating CHE and CDs will be excellent in physicochemical and functional performance by combining the functional properties of these two active components, and a new avenue for the sustainable recycling and high-value utilization of CHW can be opened by means of a multi-product cascade biorefinery strategy. To the best of our knowledge, the relevant reports are still rare.

In this study, multifunctional active packaging films were produced employing CMC as the film-forming matrix and CHE and CDs as active fillers. The effect of the added content of CDs on the physicochemical characteristics of CMC-based films in terms of optical, mechanical, gas barrier, and thermal stability was investigated. The antioxidant and antibacterial properties of CMC-based film were also evaluated. In addition, the cytotoxicity of the as-prepared films was assessed. Moreover, the fresh-keeping packaging of the obtained composite films on fresh-cut apples was explored.

Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) was obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Coffee husk waste (CHW) was obtained from EVISSA Co., Ltd. (Pu ‘er, China). The remaining chemical reagents were of analytical grade and were obtained from Tianjin Sailboat Chemical Reagent Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China).

The extraction of polyphenol from CHW was according to the method of Watrelot and Bouska (2022), with some modifications. Briefly, the dry CHW powders were extracted using the ultrasound-assisted extraction method in an ultrasonic bath (JN25-12DTS, Ningbo, China) with 80% ethanol solvents and a solid-to-liquid ratio of 1:20 (g/mL). Thereafter, the filtrate was evaporated by a rotary evaporator (Hei-VAP, Heidolph, Germany) at 40 °C to remove the ethanol, lyophilized to obtain a dry extract abundant in polyphenols (CHE), and stored at 4 °C for further experiments. Using the Folin-Ciocalteu colorimetric technique (Zou, Xi, Hu, Nie, & Zhou, 2016), the total phenolic content (TPC) of CHE was determined to be 17.5 ± 2.3 mg GAE/g as calculated.

The residue of CHW after polyphenol extraction was used to synthesize CDs by the hydrothermal method. Briefly, 4 g of dried coffee husk residue powder was mixed with 80 mL of distilled water for 8 min in an ultrasonic bath, subsequently transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel hydrothermal autoclave reactor, and sealed tightly. After six hours of heating at 200 °C, the reactor was chilled to room temperature. The mixed solution was then centrifuged for 30 min at a relative centrifugal force of 11,673 g, and the supernatant was filtered through a filter membrane (Whatman, pore diameter of 0.22 μm). Finally, the filtrate was freeze-dried to produce the CDs, which were then kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further investigation and usage.

The micro-morphology of CDs was observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai F20, Frequency Electronics, Inc., USA). The surface charge and size distribution of CDs in aqueous solutions were determined using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 analyzer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). The UV–Vis spectrum of CDs ranging from 200 to 800 nm was measured by an UV–Vis spectrophotometer (T90, Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). The fluorescence spectra of CDs were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4600, Hitachi, Japan). The chemical structure and crystallinity of the CDs were ascertained by a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer (Nicolet Is5, Thermo Electron Corporation, MA, USA) and an X-ray diffractometer (Ultima IV, Rigaku, Japan), respectively. The antioxidant and antibacterial activities of CDs were also examined, and the detailed methods were presented in the Experimental section of Supporting Information.

CMC-based composite films were produced using a solution casting method. Firstly, 100 mL of distilled water was used to dissolve 2 g of CMC, followed by the addition of 1.2 g of glycerol as a plasticizer. The resultant mixture was heated for 30 min at 70 °C while being constantly stirred. Then, 0.12 g of CHE powder (6% w/w, based on CMC) was included and magnetically agitated for 30 min to produce CMC/CHE film-forming solution (The addition amount of CHE was obtained by optimizing the mechanical strength and water vapor barrier of CMC/CHE films, the results are shown in Table S1 of Supplementary Information). Subsequently, various contents of CDs (0, 1, 3, 5, and 7% w/w, based on CMC/CHE) were added and vigorously stirred for 30 min to thoroughly disperse carbon dots for production of the CMC/CHE film-forming solution. All of the film-forming solutions were then ultrasonically treated for 30 min to eliminate bubbles. Finally, 100 g of film-forming solution was carefully poured onto a 20 × 20 cm self-made glass plate and dried indoors. The dried films were gently stripped off from the glass plates and conditioned in a humidity chamber for 48 h at 23 °C and 50% RH to balance the moisture content of the films prior to further testing. The produced films were marked as CMC/CHE, CMC/CHE/CDs1%, CMC/CHE/CDs3%, CMC/CHE/CDs5%, and CMC/CHE/CDs7%. For comparison, the pure CMC film was also prepared using the same preparation process mentioned above.

The surface micro-morphologies of the film samples were investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, MIRA LMS, Tescan, Czech). The film samples were sprayed with gold before being examined. The surface chemical groups of film samples were examined using a Nicolet Is5 FTIR spectrophotometer fitted with an attenuated total reflection (ATR) accessory. The spectral scanning was conducted in the wavelength range of 400–4000 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1. The crystallinity of film was determined using an X-ray diffractometer (Ultima IV, Rigaku Corporation, Japan) in the range of 2θ from 5° to 85° using a Cu-Kα radiation source (λ = 0.154 nm). The detailed characterization methods about physicochemical properties including mechanical, optical, photoluminescent, water resistance, gas barrier, and thermal stability of films; functional properties including antioxidant, antibacterial activity, and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation of films; and cytotoxicity of films were detailed in the Experimental section of Supporting Information.

Fresh ‘Fuji’ apples were purchased from a local supermarket in Kunming, China. The apples with uniform size without any mechanical and pest damage were selected. The apples were properly cleaned by distilled water, then peeled and sliced into 5 × 2 × 1 cm pieces. Apple slices were randomly divided into four groups and packaged in a polyethylene terephthalate (PET) box lacking a cover (Φ: 12 cm, φ: 8.5 cm, 5 cm depth). The top of the PET box was then tightly covered with polyethylene (PE), CMC, and CMC/CHE/CDs3% films. The group that did not possess packaging was the control. All samples were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. The change in visual appearance of fresh-cut apple was captured by a digital camera. Five preservation indicators, including browning index, weight loss, total soluble solids, firmness, and total bacterial counts, were measured and tracked on 0, 1, 3, 5, and 7 d. Three duplicates of the experiment were conducted. The detailed measurement methods for each indicator are shown in the Experimental section of Supporting Information.

The results of every experiment were shown as the mean ± standard deviation. A one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) following Duncan's multiple range test was conducted to determine the significance of the differences between the data. The SPSS version 26.0 package (SPSS Institute, Chicago, IL, USA) served as the tool for the analysis. The threshold for statistical significance was fixed at p < 0.05.

Representative TEM image of the synthesized CDs is presented in Fig. 1a, demonstrating that the CDs are highly monodispersed spherical shapes with a lattice spacing of ∼0.21 nm (Fig. 1b), which reveals typical graphitic structure (Oladzadabbasabadi et al., 2023). As shown in Fig. 1c, the diameters of CDs range from 1.8 to 4.2 nm (average diameters of 2.8 ± 0.03 nm), which is in accordance with the diameter range of various reported carbon dots (Oladzadabbasabadi et al., 2023; Sul et al., 2023). Further, the prepared CDs exhibited a high negative zeta potential value of −16.0 ± 4.7 mV, signifying their great stability in aqueous media.

The chemical structure and functional groups of CDs were investigated by FTIR, as shown in Fig. 1d. The absorption band around 3373 cm−1 belonged to stretching vibrations of OH and NH (Konar et al., 2019), while the peak at around 2933 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CH (Gong et al., 2023). The infrared absorption peaks at 1602, 1389 and 1048 cm−1 are due to the in-plane bending vibration of NH, the vibration of CN in the aromatic ring skeleton, and the stretching vibration of C–O–C, respectively (Mao et al., 2023). These findings demonstrated that CDs have –COOH, –OH, and nitrogenous functional groups, which could have originated from the carbohydrates and the nitrogenous compounds in CHW. A prominent diffraction peak at 2θ of 21.4° was visible in the XRD pattern of CDs (Fig. 1e), which corresponded to the diffraction of graphite carbon (Zhu, Deng, Chen, Hu, & Wu, 2023).

XPS was used to investigate the surface state and elemental composition of CDs. It can be seen from Fig. 1f that there were two dominant peaks at 285.0 and 532.0 eV, along with one weaker peak at 400.0 eV, which are associated with C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s. Moreover, the high-resolution XPS survey spectra of C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s are displayed in Fig. 1g–i. The C1s could be fitted into four peaks at 284.7, 286.1, 287.9 and 292.7 eV, which were attributed to C–C/C–H, C–O/C–N, CO and O–C=O bonds, respectively (Jeong, Park, Yi, & Yang, 2023; Zhu et al., 2023). In the high-resolution XPS survey of O1s, two peaks were observed at 531.1 and 532.6 eV attributed to CO and CO bonding respectively (Tammina & Rhim, 2023). There were three peaks in the fitted spectra of N 1 s at binding energies of 399.8, 401.8 and 406.7 eV that represented the bonds C–N–C, NH and NO, respectively (Zhu et al., 2023). The XPS results coincide well with those of the FTIR analysis.

As shown in Fig. 1j, CDs exhibited a pale-yellow tint under normal light and blue tint under UV light (365 nm) due to the quantum confinement effect, demonstrating that CDs were successfully synthesized. Moreover, the strong UV absorption peak of CDs at 278 nm should be attributed to the π → π* transition of the olefin (C=C) group in the aromatic sp2 domain (Zhu et al., 2023), indicating a high capacity for UV absorption, which can be a candidate material for the development of UV blocking active packaging films. With an excitation wavelength of 330 nm, the fluorescence (FL) spectrum of the CDs displayed the strongest FL emission at 445 nm (Fig. 1j). When the excitation wavelength increased, the FL emission likewise increased to a higher wavelength, suggesting that CDs emission depends on excitation (Fig. 1k). Additionally, it was calculated that the quantum yield (QY) of CDs was 0.66 in contrast with 0.54 QY of quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4(Fig. S2).

As displayed in Fig. 1l, the antioxidant activity of CDs was assessed using the radical scavenging rates of DPPH and ABTS. A dose-dependent increase in antioxidant activity was observed. CDs showed higher scavenging ability against ABTS radical with IC50 value of 27.1 μg/mL than that against DPPH radical with IC50 value of 45.5 μg/mL, which might be attributed to the hydrophilic character of CDs, making them better able to interact with an aqueous ABTS solution. The radical scavenging ability is attributed to the electron transfer from –OH of CDs to ABTS⋅ and DPPH⋅ radicals, resulting in quenched forms of ABTS–H and DPPH–H (Murru, Badía-Laíño, & Díaz-García, 2020). The antibacterial activity of CDs was determined by the agar well-diffusion method, as shown in Fig. 1m. The CDs exhibited good antibacterial capability with inhibition zone diameters of 5.1 mm for E. coli and 3.6 mm for L. monocytogenes, respectively.

According to Fig. 2a–f, micro-morphology of the various CMC-based film surfaces was observed by SEM. The surface of pure CMC film was flat, uniform, and compact without any cavities, cracks, or lumps (Fig. 2a). After the incorporation of CHE into the CMC matrix, the CMC/CHE film surface still maintained a homogenous and flat appearance, suggesting good compatibility between CMC and CHE (Fig. 2b). When further incorporation of CDs into the CMC/CHE matrix, the surface heterogeneity of the film occurred along with some granular substances. Among all the ternary films, the CMC/CHE/CDs1% and CMC/CHE/CDs3% exhibited relatively uniform surface micro-morphology (Fig. 2c–d), which is due to the well dispersion of CDs in the matrix. By contrast, a large number of granular substances (as pointed out by the white arrow) appeared on the CMC/CHE/CDs film surface, as the CDs content were 5 and 7% (Fig. 2e–f), attributing to the aggregation of CDs at high content.

The chemical groups of the various CMC-based films were investigated by FTIR analysis, and the results are displayed in Fig. 2g. The FTIR spectrum of pure CMC film exhibited a broad absorption peak at 3200–3300 cm−1, which corresponds with the stretching vibration of the OH group. The peaks at 2924 and 2875 cm−1 were attributed to CH stretching vibrations from methylene in CMC (Ezati, Rhim, Moradi, Tajik, & Molaei, 2020; Roy & Rhim, 2020). The peaks at 1590 and 1412 cm−1 corresponded to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the -COOH in CMC, respectively (Ezati et al., 2020). The peaks at 1103 and 1020 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of C-O-C, which are characteristic peaks of the polysaccharide skeleton (Liu et al., 2022). After the incorporation of CHE into CMC, no significant alternations in functional groups were observed in the FTIR spectra of CMC/CHE film compared with pure CMC film, except for a slight broadening and a slight red shift of the OH characteristic peak, demonstrating enhanced intermolecular interaction between CMC and CHE. The OH peak further shifted to a lower wavenumber after the addition of CDs, and the shifts became bigger and bigger until the CDs content increased to 3%, which may be attributed to the fact that the abundant -OH and -COOH groups on CDs could bind with oxygen-containing groups in CMC or CHE to generate hydrogen bonding (Khoshkalampour, Ghorbani, & Ghasempour, 2023). With the further increase in CDs content (5% and 7%), the peak of the hydroxyl group is slightly shifted to higher wavenumber. This phenomenon could potentially be attributed to decreased intramolecular interaction force resulting from CDs agglomerations, which is consistent with the SEM observations.

The crystalline architectures of various CMC-based films were analyzed by XRD, and the results are displayed in Fig. 2h. The pure CMC film exhibited a characteristic broad diffraction peak at nearly 2θ = 21.6°, indicating the presence of the CMC film as the amorphous phase (Yue et al., 2023). The CHE-added CMC film exhibited a lower intensity of the characteristic amorphous peak, illustrating that CHE facilitated the re-ordered arrangement of CMC polymer chains induced by hydrogen bonding between CMC and CHE. Furthermore, the CMC/CHE/CDs1% and CMC/CHE/CDs3% composite films further showed a relatively wide and attenuated characteristic diffraction peak of CMC, suggesting that adding small content of CDs improved the crystallization of CMC. The reason is most likely that the low content and uniform distribution of CDs in the film matrix were beneficial to the formation of more hydrogen bonds inside the molecules. Notably, the intensity of the characteristic peak slightly increased in terms of CMC/CHE/CDs5% and CMC/CHE/CDs7% films, which might be due to the aggregation of CDs at high content, which could reduce the intermolecular interaction among film components, leading to decrease in crystallinity during the film formation. These explanations aligned well with the micro-morphological and FTIR analysis presented in the previous discussion regarding the addition of CDs to CMC-based composite films.

The mechanical properties of packaging films are crucial parameters for assessing their capability to endure physical harm during transportation, preservation, and handling, as well as to maintain their integrity during packaging application. Table 1 displays the thickness, tensile strength (TS), elongation at break (EB), and elasticity modulus (EM) of the various CMC-based films. The thickness of pure CMC film is 0.041 mm, and the incorporation of CHE significantly increased the thickness of CMC/CHE film because of the more amount of solid contents in the film forming solution. The addition of CDs had no significant impact on the thickness of the CMC/CHE/CDs1% and CMC/CHE/CDs3% films, which is due to the fact that the low content of micro-sized CDs is uniformly distributed in the matrix. In comparison, the CMC/CHE/CDs5% and CMC/CHE/CDs7% films' thicknesses increased slightly as the amount of CDs increased, owing to CDs aggregation as seen in the SEM pictures. The incorporation of CHE improved the TS, EB, and EM values of the CMC/CHE film significantly, owing to the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonding between CMC and CHE. Following the CDs were added to the CMC/CHE matrix, the TS and EM values of CMC/CHE/CDs film further significantly increased (p < 0.05), while EB values decreased. The maximum TS of 46.22 MPa with EM of 1369.33 MPa is achieved for CMC/CHE/CDs3% film. The improvement in TS and EM was due to the boosted intermolecular interactions between CDs, CHE, and CMC. The abundant -OH and -COOH groups on CDs could bind with oxygen-containing groups in CMC or CHE to generate hydrogen bonding, enhancing the network rigidity of composite film and thereby resulting in stronger film. In comparison to pure CMC film, the TS value of CMC/CHE/CDs3% film increased by 130.38% and EM value improved by 236.21%. However, the EB value of CMC/CHE/CDs film was gradually declining along with the increased CDs content, which may be a result of the restricted movement of polymer molecular chains by stiff CDs nanoparticles. These observations were consistent with the report of Zhao, Zhang, Mujumdar, Adhikari, and Wang (2022). Moreover, it was found that the mechanical strength of CMC/CHE/CDs films decreased with further increasing CDs content above 3%. These phenomena could be explained by the fact that aggregation of CDs in matrix at high content weakens the interaction between nanoparticles and polymer matrix, causing phase separation among film-forming substance as observed by SEM, thereby hampering the effective stress distribution and transfer of composite films (Khoshkalampour et al., 2023; Oladzadabbasabadi et al., 2023; Wen, Li, Yan, & Su, 2023).

The color of food packaging materials has a considerable influence on consumers' intuitive perceptions of the appearance of the packaged products, the color parameters of various CMC-based films are provided in Table 2 . The pure CMC film exhibited the highest L values of 97.87, indicating its high brightness. Subsequently, the L values were reduced to a range of 90.22–94.49 upon the addition of CHE and CDs. The a and b values for CMC/CHE and CMC/CHE/CDs films were also higher than those of the pure CMC film, indicating a significant color change toward red-yellowish tinge. Consequently, the total color difference (△E) of the composite films was significantly enhanced and continued to increase with rising CDs content. The photographs in Fig. 3a show the visual appearance features of all the films overlaying a green plant. The images clearly showed that pure CMC film exhibited no color and was completely transparent, while the films containing CHE and CDs displayed a brownish hue since the inherent brown tint of CHE and CDs. Despite this, the green plant beneath these films remained clearly visible, indicating that their transparency was sufficient and acceptable.

Packaging material that has low UV-light transmittance is essential to retard nutritional loss and spoilage caused by photo-oxidation, while material with high visible-light transmittance is indispensable for see-through packaging applications. The UV–Vis light transmittance spectra of various CMC-based films that span in wavelength from 200 to 800 nm are shown in Fig. 3b. The pure CMC film possessed the highest light transmittance, which decreased dramatically when CHE was added (p < 0.05), particularly in the UV region (200–400 nm). This can be explained by the fact that the CHE containing many benzene rings and phenolic hydroxyl groups could improve the UV-light absorption by π → π* and n → π* transitions (Andrade et al., 2023). Meanwhile, the UV-light transmittance of CMC/CHE/CDs film was significantly decreased with increasing CDs content, which can be attributed to CDs capable of absorbing UV light, as previously mentioned. The light transmittance at 280 nm (T280) and 600 nm (T600) was determined to assess the UV-light blocking capability and transparency of the film samples. T280 and T600 values of pure CMC film were 75.7% and 90.3%, respectively, which significantly (p < 0.05) decreased to 14.0% and 86.2% after addition of CHE, significantly (p < 0.05) reduced by 92.3–100% and 4.5–8.1% after further introduction of CDs. It could be found that the decrease in T280 is more prominent than that in T600, revealing that the addition of CHE and CDs imparts a significantly greater barrier effect for UV light with only a slight sacrifice of visible-light transmittance. In short, CMC/CHE/CDs films can exert an exceptional UV barrier property without compromising the visibility, showing promising application in UV-light shielding packaging.

Considering the intriguing fluorescence of CDs, it is anticipated that the composite film containing CDs will possess photoluminescent property. As shown in Fig. 3c, the matching visual photographs of the CMC/CHE/CDs composite films showed that a sky-blue emission can be seen under UV light (365 nm) as opposed to yellow light under visible light. Fig. 3d shows the FL emission and excitation spectra for CMC/CHE/CDs film-forming solutions. The strongest emission peak was located at 401 nm with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. Notably, the FL spectrum of the film solutions exhibits a modest blue shift in both the excitation peak and the emission peak compared to the CDs (Fig. 1j), which may be due to the interaction between CDs and the CMC/CHE matrix. The FL spectra of all CMC/CHE/CDs films displayed a single peak at 401 nm, and its intensity increased as the CDs content rose, as shown in Fig. 3e. These results provide evidence that the addition of CDs endowed CMC/CHE/CDs films with photoluminescent properties.

WS and WCA, tightly related to the hydrophilicity property of the material, are regarded as crucial functional characteristics for biopolymer-based films (Mousazadeh, Ehsani, Moghaddas Kia, & Ghasempour, 2021). In Fig. 4a–b, the WS and WCA for various films are displayed. The pure CMC film exhibited the highest WS of 87.40% and lowest WCA of 41.72° (Boro, Priyadarsini, & Moholkar, 2022), attributing to the inherent hydrophilicity of CMC. The incorporation of CHE significantly reduced (p < 0.05) the WS and increased the WCA of the CMC/CHE film, which might be because the majority of the phenolic hydroxyl groups in CHE can react with the CMC matrix through hydrogen bonds (Tagrida, Nilsuwan, Gulzar, Prodpran, & Benjakul, 2023). Following the introduction of CDs into the CMC/CHE matrix, the WS value decreased initially and then increased, while the WCA value exhibited an opposite change trend. Among them, CMC/CHE/CDs3% film displayed the best water resistance, with WS of 44.11% and WCA of 75.70°. These results can be explained by the fact that the incorporation of low-content CDs into the film matrix induced the formation of compact structure of composite film through hydrogen bonds among CDs, CMC and CHE, thus consuming free hydrophilic groups in the films and enhancing water resistance of films. Whereas the amount of free hydrophilic groups in the composite films increased owing to CDs agglomeration at high content, thereby decreasing water resistance of films.

It is crucial for food packing materials to maintain a suitable moisture and gas environment for food preservation. A low WVP value could effectively decrease the moisture exchange between food and surroundings, while a low OP value could prevent food oxidation and spoilage. Fig. 4c–d displays the WVP and OP values of various films. The pure CMC film exhibited the lowest barrier to water vapor and oxygen, with a WVP value of 2.84 × 10−10 g·m/m2·Pa·s and an OP value of 9.81 cm3/m2·24 h·0.1 MPa, while the WVP and OP values drastically decreased to 2.17 × 10−10 g·m/m2·Pa·s and 9.15 cm3/m2·24 h·0.1 MPa with the addition of CHE into the CMC matrix. This was because abundant phenolic hydroxyl groups in CHE can interact with CMC to form a dense structure (SEM images) through hydrogen bonds, thus hindering the infiltration of water and oxygen molecules. With the further incorporation of CDs, the WVP and OP of the CMC/CHE/CDs films first decreased and then increased. At low-content CDs (1 and 3%), the gas barrier of CMC/CHE/CDs films enhanced with the increasing CDs content, a minimum WVP value of 1.60 × 10−10 g·m/m2·Pa·s and an OP value of 5.21 cm3/m2·24 h·0.1 MPa could be achieved for CMC/CHE/CDs3% films. The reason might be attributed to the creation of circuitous passages for gas in the composite films by the uniform distribution of CDs. The subsequent reduction in gas barrier might be on account of the aggregation effect of CDs at high content (5 and 7%). Moreover, the change trend of the gas barrier was also supported by XRD analysis, where impenetrable crystalline regions of composite films increased at low CDs content, then reduced at high CDs content.

The thermal stability of various CMC-based composite films was assessed using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), and the resulting TGA and derivative thermogravimetric (DTG) curves can be seen in Fig. S3. All the films exhibited a distinct two-step thermal degradation process. The first thermal degradation occurred between 60 and 130 °C because of the moisture evaporation in the films. The second degradation that happened between 240 and 330 °C was caused by the thermal degradation of CMC polymer chains and carbon-based structures. The maximum thermal degradation temperature (Tm) of CMC, CMC/CHE, and CMC/CHE/CDs1–7% films was 281.4 °C, 287.2 °C, 290.7 °C, 294.2 °C, 289.5 °C, and 285.4 °C, respectively. The weight loss rates of these films at the second stage were 47.1%, 46.4%, 45.8%, 45.3%, 46.1%, and 46.7%, respectively. These results revealed that the incorporation of low-content CDs (<3%) had a positive effect on the thermal stability of the composite films. The primary explanation given was that low-content CDs can form a tight assembly with the polymer matrix through intermolecular forces, while high-content CDs agglomeration leads to a weaker force. The results were consistent with those obtained by SEM and FTIR analyses. The same results on CDs-added films have been reported in other research (Gong et al., 2024).

The antioxidant activity of food packaging films is of great significance to reduce the adverse effects of free radicals on the nutritional composition of food, extending the shelf life of packaged food. Fig. 5a displays the results of the antioxidant properties of CMC-based films assessed by scavenging DPPH and ABTS radicals. The pure CMC film exhibited extremely weak antioxidant activities, with DPPH and ABTS scavenging rates of 3.7% and 2.0%, respectively. By contrast, the DPPH and ABTS scavenging activities of the CMC/CHE composite film significantly (p < 0.05) increased to 59.8% and 90.4%, respectively, mainly owing to the presence of phenolic hydroxyl groups that have the ability to eliminate free radicals by producing phenoxy groups (Zeb, 2021). Furthermore, the antioxidant properties of CMC/CHE/CDs films were greatly enhanced with the increased CDs, and the scavenging rates against DPPH of 95.1% and ABTS of 98.6% were already achieved for the CMC/CHE/CDs3% film. It was noteworthy that all the films presented higher scavenging ability against ABTS radicals than that against DPPH radical, which is due to the aqueous ABTS solution resulting in greater interaction among free radicals and hydrophilic CDs. Hence, these results demonstrated that integration of CHE and CDs into the CMC matrix could endow the films with outstanding antioxidant activities, showing promising potential in retarding the oxidation and deterioration of food.

Antibacterial packaging is extensively employed to mitigate food spoilage and foodborne diseases resulting from microbial growth or contamination during storage and transit. The antibacterial efficacy of many film samples against gram-positive (L. monocytogenes) and gram-negative (E. coli) bacteria was investigated using the total colony counting method; the results are displayed in Fig. 5b. Pure CMC film had negligible antibacterial effect, whereas CMC/CHE film demonstrated modest antibacterial activities against both types of bacteria, showing 46.6% antibacterial efficiency against E. coli and 51.2% against L. monocytogenes. The improvement in antibacterial activity of CMC/CHE film may be due to the antibacterial capability of phenolic compounds in CHE, which cause physiological changes in microbial cell membranes, finally leading to bacterial death (Riaz et al., 2018). As expected, antibacterial efficiency of CMC/CHE/CDs films significantly improved when antibacterial CDs were further introduced to the CMC/CHE matrix. The antibacterial efficiency of CMC/CHE/CDs films displayed a CDs content-dependent manner. The antibacterial efficiency of CMC/CHE/CDs3–7% films against E. coli and L. monocytogenes was 93.4% and 82.2%, 99.9% and 92.7%, 100% and 100%, respectively. According to these findings, CMC/CHE/CDs films showed good antibacterial characteristics against both gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria, making them suitable for use in active food packaging. Notably, the lower antibacterial efficiency found in the CMC/CHE/CDs films against L. monocytogenes can be attributed to the existence of thick and rigid peptidoglycan layers in the L. monocytogenes cell wall, which act as a barrier to resist external attack.

Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are a class of chemical substances that include extremely active oxygen atoms. These compounds include the hydroxyl radical (⋅OH), singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide anions (⋅O2 −), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (Zhou, Cai, He, Tang, & Lu, 2024). It is widely recognized that the primary antibacterial mechanism of CDs is the generation of ROS, which leads to oxidative stress in bacteria and inhibits growth. ⋅OH and ⋅O2 − are the most common species of ROS. Terephthalic acid (TPA) can combine with ⋅OH to produce fluorescent 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (HTPA), and as a result, the FL intensity of HTPA can be used to determine the generation of ⋅OH (Zhao et al., 2023). As displayed in Fig. 5c, the pure CMC and CMC/CHE films did not produce HTPA, whereas those of the CMC/CHE/CDs films showed that HTPA was produced and that the FL intensity of HTPA increased as CDs were added, indicating that ⋅OH generation is proportional to the content of CDs in the CMC/CHE/CDs film. Since the reaction between fluorescent 1, 3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) and ⋅O2 − generated non-fluorescent 1, 2-dibenzoyl benzene, the decrease in FL of DPBF indicates the production of ⋅O2 − (Wang et al., 2023). In Fig. 5d, the FL of DPBF of both CMC and CMC/CHE films was the highest and nearly unchanged, while that of CMC/CHE/CDs films gradually declined within 6 min, and the decline rate accelerated with the increased CDs content. These results indicated that the CMC and CMC/CHE films did not generate ⋅O2 − within 6 min, while the films incorporating CDs generated ⋅O2 − continuously, and the generation of ⋅O2 − increased as the CDs content rose.

It is necessary to consider the cytotoxicity of the materials when determining the practical potential of food packaging films (Bi, Tian, Zhang, Hao, & Hou, 2021). The MTT assay has been employed to determine the in vitro cytotoxicity of pure CMC film and CMC/CHE/CDs3% film against L929 cells. As shown in Fig. 5e, the cell viability was not significantly reduced (p > 0.05) compared to the control group as the concentration of the film extracts increased from 12.5 to 100 μg/mL. The cell viability kept above 95% even when co-culturing with 100 μg/mL of film extracts. These findings demonstrated that CMC film and CMC/CHE/CDs3% composite film had low cytotoxicity and were safe for food packaging applications. Considering the in vivo cytotoxicity of nanomaterials in food packaging still merits further study, so we will continue to discuss the issue in the future.

Fresh-cut apples, as a kind of ready-to-eat fruit and vegetable product, have gradually entered the vision of consumers with the change of in consumers' lifestyles and the rapid development of cold chain logistics. However, the edible life of fresh-cut apples is frequently short (about 1 day at 4 °C) due to mechanical damage, nutrient loss, browning, and other factors that restrict the development of the fresh-cut apple industry. In this work, various films were used to preserve fresh-cut apples in order to investigate the potential of the resulting composite film in food packaging.

Fig. 6a–b showed the impacts of various packaging films on the visual appearance and browning degree of fresh-cut apples during storage. Before storage, all fresh-cut apples were plump, white, and well-hydrated. All groups displayed varied degrees of browning as storage days increased. It is well known that the oxidation of phenolic substances in fruits causes the production of quinones, which are highly reactive and create melanin, thus leading to the browning of fruits. Compared with the packaged groups, the fresh-cut apples in the control group exhibited the most severe shrinkage and the darkest color with the highest BI value. The packaged groups were ranked according to BI values as follows: CMC film >PE film > CMC/CHE/CDs3% film. The decreased BI values can be due to the good gas barrier of these two films. The lowest BI values in the CMC/CHE/CDs3% film group were also associated with its good antioxidant activity, which effectively reduced the generation of melanin. Similar results were observed by Chen et al. (2021), who packaged fresh-cut apples with antioxidant thymol/alginate films.

Fresh-cut apples packaged with various films gradually lose water during preservation because of respiration and water evaporation (Fig. 6c). On day 7, 19.0% weight loss rate was found in the control group, which was significantly greater (p < 0.05) than that of the packaged groups (5.0%–11.8%). This difference was due to the barrier of packaging materials to some extent. Among the packaged groups, the fresh-cut apples in PE groups exhibited the smallest weight loss rate of only 5.0% at the end of storage, owing to the excellent water vapor barrier of PE film. However, <10.0% weight loss was observed in the CMC/CHE/CDs3% film group on day 7 due to its moderate water vapor barrier, which was lower than that in the CMC film group (11.8% weight loss).

TSS is a crucial parameter for determining the sweetness and nutritional value in apple flavor. Fig. 6d shows the changes of TSS in various groups of fresh-cut apples during preservation. The TSS of apple samples in all groups gradually decreased during storage. The reason was that carbohydrates served as the primary fuels for respiration during storage, resulting in a reduction in sugars (Zhang et al., 2021). It can be seen that fresh-cut apples packaged with CMC/CHE/CDs3% film had the highest TSS values during the whole preservation, indicating that the CMC/CHE/CDs3% film was more effective in delaying the consumption of soluble solids, preserving the quality of fresh-cut apples. This might be related to the superior oxygen barrier of the CMC/CHE/CDs3% film, which can inhibit the consumption of soluble solids caused by cellular respiration.

Firmness also is one of important criterion for determining fresh-cut apples quality and freshness. The impact of various packages on the firmness of fresh-cut apples is displayed in Fig. 6e. The firmness of fresh-cut apples in all groups decreased steadily with the duration of storage. The three main factors that cause fresh-cut apples to soften are respiration, deformation of cell walls by water loss or by cell wall degrading enzymes, and structural destruction caused by bacterial growth (Xie et al., 2022). At the end of preservation, the firmness of fresh-cut apples in the control group was the lowest (12.5 N), and the firmness of fresh-cut apples in the CMC film, PE film, and CMC/CHE/CDs3% film groups was 28.8, 32.2, and 33.5 N, respectively, relating to the presence of packaging protection. It was found that the firmness of the CMC/CHE/CDs3% film group maintained the higher level during the entire preservation period among all the packaged groups, which was due to the synergistic effect of the good gas barrier and antibacterial properties of the CMC/CHE/CDs3% film.

Bacterial reproduction and growth can consume the nutrients in fresh-cut apples, reducing their nutritional value and shelf-life. Fig. 6f displays the TBC values of fresh-cut apples packaged with various films during the entire preservation. The initial TBC value of fresh-cut apples was 2.1 logCFUg−1, and the TBC values of all groups progressively increased during storage. According to Wu, Zhang, Bhandari, and Guo (2021), the acceptable TBC limit for fresh-cut fruits was 6.0 logCFUg−1. On the 7th day, the TBC in the control group increased to 6.2 logCFUg−1 due to the infection by external bacteria, exceeding the acceptable limit of 6.0 logCFUg−1, indicating their inedibility. Although PE film has a certain protective effect, the TBC value (6.1 logCFUg−1) in the PE group still exceeded the limit value at the end of storage. This is since stronger water vapor barrier of PE film contributed to the generation of a high-humidity atmosphere inside the packaging, thereby inducing the rapid growth and reproduction of bacteria. Fortunately, the TBC values in the CMC film and CMC/CHE/CDs3% film groups remained within the threshold range due to their moderate water vapor barrier. Especially, the TBC value of the CMC/CHE/CDs3% film group was only 4.1 logCFUg−1 on day 7, thanks to its good antibacterial activity. In conclusion, in terms of appearance, nutritional, physicochemical, and microbiological indicators of fresh-cut apples, the CMC/CHE/CDs3% film could effectively prolong the shelf-life of fresh-cut apples up to 7 days at 4 °C.

In this study, CMC-based composite films were successfully produced using the solution casting method with 6% (w/w) CHE and various contents of CDs (0, 1, 3, 5, and 7%, w/w) as active fillers. The synthesized CDs had a particle size range of 1.8 to 4.2 nm and a typical graphite structure with strong antioxidant and antibacterial properties. The incorporation of various contents of CDs improved the water resistance, gas barrier, antioxidant, and antibacterial properties of the CMC-based multifunctional films and also endowed the films with UV-light blocking and photoluminescent properties. Among them, the CDs loading of 3% exhibited the best water resistance, mechanical, and gas barrier properties. Therefore, the CMC/CHE/CDs3% film with comprehensive physicochemical and functional characteristics was used for fresh-cut apple preservation. The CMC/CHE/CDs3% film effectively extended the shelf life of fresh-cut apples by preventing browning, weight loss, nutrient loss, softening, and limiting bacterial growth. Overall, CMC/CHE/CDs film had promising applications in the food packaging business and helped to the growth of a sustainable and eco-friendly bioeconomy.

Junxian Yang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yongshi Li: Validation, Investigation. Bingzhen Liu: Supervision, Software. Kun Wang: Supervision, Data curation. Hui Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Lincai Peng: Supervision, Software, Methodology, Conceptualization.
